## {background-color="#533860"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.8em; font-weight: bold;"}
📊 Psychometrics
:::
::: {style="color: #FFE42A; font-size: 1.1em;"}
Measuring the unmeasurable
:::

## What Does Correlation Mean? {#correlation-intuition}

**When X goes up, Y tends to go ___**

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Pearson r**

- Measures linear relationship
- Ranges from –1 to +1
- r = 0: no linear pattern
- r = 1: perfect positive line
- r = –1: perfect negative line
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Real examples**

- Height & weight: r ≈ 0.5
- Hours studied & grade: r ≈ 0.4
- Exercise & resting heart rate: r ≈ –0.3
:::

::::

::: {.fragment}
> Scatter plots: imagine a cloud of points — tighter cloud = stronger r
:::

## The Limits of Correlation {#correlation-limits}

**r = ±1 requires a perfectly linear relationship**

::: {.fragment}
- If one variable is **binary** (0/1), r can *never* reach ±1
:::
::: {.fragment}
- Why? Only two possible X-values → scatter pattern is constrained
:::
::: {.fragment}
- Maximum |r| between binary and continuous depends on base rate p
:::
::: {.fragment}
$$\text{max}|r_{\text{point-biserial}}| \propto \sqrt{p(1-p)}$$

- p = 0.5 → highest possible max |r|
- p = 0.1 or 0.9 → max |r| shrinks substantially
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Survey items are often binary or ordinal — this matters!**
:::

## Types of Correlation in Psychometrics {#correlation-types}

**Different variable types → different correlation methods**

| Variable Combo | Method |
|---|---|
| Continuous × Binary | **Point-biserial** r |
| Continuous × Ordinal | **Polyserial** |
| Binary × Binary | **Tetrachoric** |
| Ordinal × Ordinal | **Polychoric** |

::: {.fragment}
- Most psych scales use **Likert items** (ordinal)
- Polychoric is the workhorse of psychometrics
- Using Pearson r on ordinal data **underestimates** true relationship
:::

::: {.fragment}
```r
library(polycor)
cor(x, y)         # Pearson — underestimates on ordinal
polychor(x, y)    # Polychoric — assumes latent normal
```
:::

## The Latent Variable Assumption {#latent-variable}

**What polychoric/tetrachoric actually assume**

::: {.fragment}
- Observed categories = **discretization** of a hidden continuous variable
- Latent variable assumed to be **normally distributed**
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Example: "Rate your pain 1–5"**

- Your actual pain exists on a continuous scale
- You cross a threshold → you report the next category up
:::

::: {.fragment}
:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
Hidden number line:

`——|——|——|——|——→`

`  1   2   3   4   5`

Thresholds (τ) carve it into bins
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
- We only observe which bin
- Latent value is inferred
- Polychoric recovers the **latent correlation**
:::

::::
:::

## Factor Analysis vs PCA {#factor-analysis}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
**PCA**

- Decomposes *total* variance
- Finds directions of max variance
- Components explain a bit of everything
- No noise/signal separation
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Factor Analysis**

- Decomposes *shared* variance
- Finds latent constructs
- Factors explain specific clusters
- Separates signal from noise
:::

::::

::: {.fragment}
**FA assumes**: variance = common + unique + error

**Classic example**: Reading, math, verbal all load on latent **"g"** (general intelligence)

FA recovers this structure; PCA just rotates the data
:::

## Item Response Theory (IRT) {#irt}

**Models probability of a correct/positive response**

::: {.fragment}
Two key parameters per item:

- **Difficulty (β)**: where on ability scale does P(correct) = 0.5?
- **Discrimination (α)**: how steeply does the curve rise?
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Item Characteristic Curve (ICC)**:

$$P(\text{correct} \mid \theta) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\alpha(\theta - \beta)}}$$

S-shaped logistic curve, θ = person's latent ability
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Advantages over classical test theory**

- Item parameters don't depend on the sample
- Person ability doesn't depend on which items used
- Used by: GRE, SAT, LSAT, AP exams
:::

## The Big Caveat {#psych-caveat}

**All of this assumes (at least latent) continuity**

::: {.fragment}
- What if variables are **truly categorical**?
  - Not discretized — genuinely unordered
  - Polychoric/IRT assumptions break down
:::

::: {.fragment}
- Correlations and factor loadings become **misleading**
  - Artificially inflated or deflated
  - Factors may reflect response style, not constructs
:::

::: {.fragment}
- **Open questions**:
  - Are Likert responses truly ordinal?
  - Is "strongly agree → agree" the same distance as "agree → neutral"?
  - When does misspecification affect conclusions enough to matter?
:::

::: {.fragment}
> Active area of research — no settled answer
:::

---

[← Back to Topics](#toc)
